items.q: Why do TNCs locate production in LICs?
items.correct: Lower labour costs, weaker regulation, access to raw materials, large potential markets, and government incentives like tax breaks in special economic zones.
items.misconception: Because TNCs want to develop poorer countries and lift them out of poverty.
items.random_wrong: Because LICs have better infrastructure than HICs.
items.why: Students attribute moral motives. TNCs are profit-driven; any development benefit is a side effect, not a goal — a critical distinction for evaluation questions.

items.q: Why can a cold winter NOT disprove climate change?
items.correct: Climate is the long-term statistical average; a single cold winter is a weather event. Climate change is the trend in averages and extremes over decades, not the absence of cold days.
items.misconception: A really cold winter proves global warming is not happening.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only happens in summer.
items.why: This is one of the most common public misconceptions. Climate is the slope of the trend over 30+ years; individual weather events are scatter around that line — both can be cold.

items.q: What is the difference between a megacity and a world city?
items.correct: A megacity is defined by population (over 10 million); a world city is defined by global economic and cultural influence (e.g. New York, London) — so a city can be one without the other.
items.misconception: Megacities and world cities are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A megacity has over 1 million people and a world city has over 5 million.
items.why: Dhaka and Kinshasa are megacities but not world cities; Zurich is a world city but not a megacity. The distinction is quantitative (size) vs qualitative (function and global connections).
It's GATT. “OECD” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 1995. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
